title: Committee for the Naming of Roundabouts

fields.Dutch name: Commissie voor de Naamgeving van Rotondes (CNR)
fields.Parent ministry: Ministry of Infrastructure and Water Management

content.text: The **Committee for the Naming of Roundabouts** (Dutch: *Commissie voor de Naamgeving van Rotondes*, **CNR**) is an advisory body of the [Dutch government](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Government_of_the_Netherlands) charged with reviewing, approving, and — where necessary — rejecting proposed names for [roundabouts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roundabout) in the [Netherlands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Netherlands). Established in 1987 by ministerial decree under the [Ministry of Transport and Public Works](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ministry_of_Infrastructure_and_Water_Management), the CNR operates from offices in [Amersfoort](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amersfoort) and is composed of nine appointed members and four ex officio representatives from relevant government agencies.[^1] The Committee has been described by the *Volkskrant* as "the most quietly powerful body in Dutch infrastructure" and by its own former chair as "a necessary guardian against toponymic chaos."
History
Background and establishment

The Netherlands experienced a dramatic expansion of roundabout construction during the 1980s, as municipal traffic planners embraced the design as a safer and more efficient alternative to signalised intersections. Between 1980 and 1986, the number of roundabouts in the country rose from approximately 60 to over 400. This rapid proliferation was accompanied by an equally rapid and entirely uncoordinated naming process. Municipalities named roundabouts after local dignitaries, historical events, geographical features, and — in several documented cases — the personal pets of sitting aldermen.

The tipping point came in late 1986, when the municipality of [Oss](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oss) approved the name *Fikkie-rotonde* for a newly constructed roundabout on the N329, honouring a Jack Russell terrier belonging to wethouder Henk Molenaar that had been struck by a car at the same location in 1983. A formal complaint by the regional chapter of the *ANWB* argued that the name was "an affront to the dignity of public infrastructure." The ensuing media coverage — widely referred to as the **Fikkie-affaire** — prompted the Minister of Transport, **Neelie Smit-Kroes**, to establish the CNR by ministerial decree on 8 March 1987.[^2]
Early years and the Naming Code

The Committee's first act was the publication of the *Leidraad Rotondenaamgeving* ("Roundabout Naming Code") in September 1987, a 94-page document establishing formal criteria for acceptable names. The Code specified that roundabout names must satisfy the following conditions:

items: The name must bear a demonstrable historical, geographical, or cultural connection to the immediate surroundings (within a radius of 2 kilometres)
The named individual, if applicable, must have been deceased for a minimum of 10 years (reduced from the originally proposed 25 years after parliamentary objection)
The name must not duplicate any existing roundabout name within the same province
The name must be pronounceable in standard Dutch without ambiguity, as determined by a phonetic assessment panel
The name must not, in the judgment of the Committee, invite ridicule or diminish public confidence in the road network

content.text: The final criterion — colloquially known within the CNR as the **lachcriterium** ("laughter criterion") — has proven the most controversial and the most frequently invoked basis for rejection.
Organisational structure

The CNR consists of thirteen members. Nine are appointed by the Minister of Infrastructure and Water Management for renewable four-year terms. Appointees are typically drawn from the fields of urban planning, Dutch history, linguistics, and civil engineering. The four ex officio members represent the [Kadaster](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kadaster), the [Rijkswaterstaat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rijkswaterstaat), the *Vereniging van Nederlandse Gemeenten* (VNG), and the *Koninklijk Nederlands Aardrijkskundig Genootschap* (KNAG).

The Committee convenes on the second Thursday of every month in its offices at the Stationsstraat in Amersfoort, with the exception of July and August. Meeting minutes are published in the *Staatscourant* within 30 working days, though in practice the delay has at times exceeded six months, a fact noted with disapproval by the *Algemene Rekenkamer* in its 2019 audit.
Notable naming disputes
The Nijntje-rotonde (2003)

In 2003, the municipality of [Utrecht](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Utrecht) proposed naming a new roundabout near the Centraal Museum after [Nijntje](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miffy) (Miffy), the fictional rabbit created by Utrecht-born artist [Dick Bruna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dick_Bruna). The CNR rejected the application on the grounds that Bruna, then still living, did not meet the 10-year posthumous requirement. A second objection noted that Nijntje, being a fictional rabbit, could not be said to have "died" at all and therefore existed in a permanent state of naming ineligibility. The municipality resubmitted the application in 2022, following Bruna's death in 2017, and it was approved after a 14-month review.[^3]
The Rotonde van de Toekomst dispute (2011)

The city of [Almere](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Almere) submitted a proposal to name a roundabout *Rotonde van de Toekomst* ("Roundabout of the Future"), arguing that the name reflected Almere's identity as a forward-looking planned city. The CNR rejected the proposal on the grounds that the name lacked a concrete referent and would become "increasingly inaccurate with each passing year." The decision was appealed to the *Raad van State*, which upheld the Committee's ruling in a landmark 2013 judgment that established the legal precedent that roundabout names must refer to verifiable past or present entities, not aspirational concepts.
The Eredivisie-rotonde crisis (2016)

In 2016, twelve municipalities simultaneously submitted proposals to name roundabouts after recently deceased football legend [Johan Cruijff](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Johan_Cruyff). The CNR approved three applications — in [Amsterdam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amsterdam), [Betondorp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Betondorp), and [Barcelona](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barcelona) (in an honorary advisory capacity) — but rejected the remaining nine, citing the provincial non-duplication rule. The rejected municipalities accused the Committee of favouring the Randstad, prompting a heated *Tweede Kamer* debate in which MP Geert Wilders described the CNR as "a bureaucratic traffic island of its own."
Annual reports and statistics
The CNR publishes an annual report (*Jaarverslag*) detailing submission volumes, approval rates, and thematic trends. The following table summarises key figures from recent reporting periods:

content.caption: CNR annual submission and approval statistics (selected years)

content.text: The 2022 report noted a sharp increase in submissions featuring names related to internet culture, social media influencers, and cryptocurrency brands — all of which were rejected. The Committee observed in its annual summary that "the roundabout, as a permanent feature of the built environment, is ill-suited to the memorialisation of phenomena whose cultural half-life may be measured in months."
A roundabout is not a tweet. It is concrete, asphalt, and public trust. Its name must endure longer than a trending topic.
content.attribution: Mr. drs. Pieter-Jan Hoekstra, Chair of the CNR, Jaarverslag 2022

content.text: Proposed dissolution

In October 2022, the Ministry of Infrastructure and Water Management published a policy memorandum proposing the dissolution of the CNR as part of a broader effort to reduce the number of advisory bodies within the ministry. The memorandum argued that roundabout naming could be devolved entirely to municipalities without meaningful loss of quality or consistency. The proposal triggered an unexpectedly vigorous public response: within three weeks, a petition to preserve the Committee gathered over 40,000 signatures, and the *Amersfoortse Courant* ran a front-page editorial titled "Handen af van de CNR" ("Hands off the CNR"). As of 2024, the dissolution proposal remains under review, and the Committee continues to meet monthly.[^4]
